The command surfaces are enough to see which runs are fully call-reviewed, which ones still have partial segment coverage, and which ones are dominated by high-severity local issues. It also shows that the aggregate is anchored to a specific segmentation basis, so some segment artifacts are intentionally rejected instead of merged.

Call coverage is complete in the displayed runs, while segment coverage is the bottleneck. `tokio-rs__tokio-5200` is a representative degraded case: only 1/5 segments are usable and most rows are anchor mismatches.

The subtle part is that high `Issues` does not automatically mean better or worse overall quality; it often reflects concentrated search thrash inside otherwise fully covered runs. Another non-obvious point is that `Skips` are not missing data in the ordinary sense; they are artifacts that exist but do not match the selected anchor basis.

The corpus looks skewed toward heavily iterated troubleshooting sessions with lots of repeated search/read behavior, especially in the high-issue runs. The dataset is structurally richer than the current graph shows, but it is also version-sensitive, so one segmentation pass is not stable enough to treat as universal truth.

`Calls` is reviewed/total tool calls. `Segments` is usable segment reviews/total segments against the selected anchor basis, `Issues` is the number of high-signal call-review rows being counted for the run, `Gaps` are missing segment indices under the current aggregate view, `Skips` are segment artifacts rejected because they do not match the chosen anchor basis, and `Summary` is a compact call-coverage versus segment-usability bar.

You can derive cross-run rank order, completeness, the concentration of search-thrash-like issues, and whether a run is healthy versus structurally degraded. You can also infer when a run is fully call-covered but still analytically weak because segment review coverage is thin or mismatched.

The small graphs are distribution summaries, not new metrics. `Coverage shape` is the segment-coverage profile across the selected rows, and `Issue severity` is the severity distribution of the selected call issues; the histograms below them show how concentrated the values are.

It is reasonable to assume the current anchor segmentation is the intended reference and that cross-run comparison should privilege usability over raw artifact count. It is also reasonable to assume high-severity issues are mostly local workflow friction rather than product bugs, because the issue labels cluster around search thrash and partial next steps.

The biggest unlocks would be: issue-type breakdown by count and severity, the exact anchor segmentation identity/version for each run, change-over-time between anchors or reruns, a per-run normalization for issue density per call or turn, and a direct mapping from issue rows back to the underlying call sequence or segment spans. Those would turn the table from a triage surface into an intervention-ranking surface.

The least meaningful current element is the coarse sparkline-like summary, because it compresses too much structure into a tiny glyph strip. The next least useful is the generic `Signal histograms` label, because the reader has to infer that it is really segment-review usability plus issue severity, not a broader protocol signal model.

I would prioritize fixing segment-anchor stability and making the issue categories explicit before chasing more aggregation polish. The data suggests repeated search thrash is a dominant failure mode, so the system should surface it earlier, reduce repeated dead-end search loops, and make mismatched segmentation easy to spot.

I would replace the generic `Signal histograms` section with explicit labels and counts for segment-review usability versus call-issue severity. That would make the graphs legible without requiring the reader to infer what the buckets mean.
I would keep the compact all-runs table with coverage and summary bars. It is the right density for a 100-column terminal and it gives a fast triage map without forcing a scroll-heavy report.
